LAW DEPARTMENT 
Michael J. Baker, Esq.

Dear Mr. Baker:

August 5, 1977 
United States Steel Corporation established in 1975, pursuant 
to the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971, as amended (the "Federal 
Act"), a separate segregated fund named the USS Employees Good Govern-
ment Fund (the "Fund"). 
Contributions to the Fund may not be "earmarked." 
The Fund has been utilized primarily to support candidates for Federal 
office; however, consideration is now being given to utilizing the Fund 
to support candidates for state and local office in California. 
In this 
connection, I would like to raise for your consideration several points. 
1. 
When would the Fund become a "committee"? It is clear 
that once the Fund comes within any of the definitions of "committee" in 
Government Code Section 82013 then it must satisfy the reporting require-
ments of Sections 84200 et~. However, since there is a substantial 
balance of funds in the Fund at any given time, it is a bit unclear how 
the definition of Section 82l03(a) would be applied. 
Until the Fund is 
utilized in connection with California elections, it cannot be said to 
have received any "contributions" as that term is used in Section 82l03(a). 
On the other hand, once it has been so utilized, all of the balance of 
funds on hand might be considered "contributions." A sensible approach, 
it seems to me, would be to conclude that, until a total of $500 or more 
in a calendar year is spent from the Fund in connection with California 
elections, the Fund would not be considered to have received $500 or more 
in contributions and, hence, it would not be a committee as defined in 
Section 82l03(a). 
Upon expending $500 or more in a calendar year in 
connection with California elections, the Fund would become a "committee." 
2. 
When would the first "cumulative" period commence? 
Another 
problem arises out of the definition of "cumulative amount" in Government 
Code Section 82018. 
Is it correct that the period utilized to calculate 
the cumulative amount for purposes of filing the first series of reports 
following registration of the Fund would commence with the date of the 
first expenditure from the Fund in connection with California elections 
made during the calendar year in which the Fund was so registered? 
Or 
would such period commence with the date the Fund becomes a "committee"? 


Michael J. Baker, Esq. 
August 5, 1977 
Page -2-
3. 
Might the Fund, if limited to support in connection with 
November elections, aggregate on a calendar-year basis? 
Yet another 
concern is that the periods during which contributions and expenditures 
are aggregated begin with the closing date of the last previously-filed 
post election report. 
Currently this information is stored electronically 
and the aggregating is done on a calendar-year basis (as is required by the 
Federal Act). 
Is it permissible for the post election report following 
each November election to be filed with a closing date of the following 
December 31st if the Fund is not utilized in connection with California 
elections for the period between that election and the following January I? 
Of course, during that period the Fund will be receiving contributions. 
If 
such were allowed, and the Fund was utilized only in connection with Novem-
ber elections, the periods for aggregating would always commence with 
January 1 of the current year. 
4. 
May administrative costs of the Fund be allocated if other 
contributions are not? 
With respect to the information to be reported, 
I note Mr. Kenneth Finney's letter of September 15, 1976 to Mr. Thomas I. 
McKnew, Jr. of the Atcheson, Topeka & Santa Fe Railway Company. Mr. Finney 
concludes therein that Civic Trust 80 need report only that portion of 
contributions to it as are allocated to California. 
The method of 
allocation suggested was based on the ratio of total contributions received 
to expenditures made in connection with California elections. 
This ratio, 
of course, constantly changes as expenditures are made and contributions 
received. 
During a period utilized to calculate a "cumulative amount" the 
ratio may change from, say, 10% to 20%. 
Would an individual who contributed 
$400 at the start of that period have to be identified as a contributor of 
$50 or more? 
If post election reports can be filed through December 31, it 
may be easier for the Fund to fully report contributions and expenditures 
without any allocation to California. 
Would this be permitted? If contri-
butions and expenditures are fully reported without any allocation to 
California, may the costs of administering the Fund and the value of 
services provided to the Fund by U. S. Steel nevertheless be allocated in 
the manner outlined in Mr. Finney's letter to Mr. McKnew? 
Attorney 
REH:blw

sections.question: 1. When would the Fund become a "committee"? Until the Fund is utilized in connection with California elections, it cannot be said to have received any "contributions" as that term is used in Section 82103(a). A sensible approach would be to conclude that, until a total of $500 or more in a calendar year is spent from the Fund in connection with California elections, the Fund would not be considered to have received $500 or more in contributions. 2. When would the first "cumulative" period commence? The period utilized to calculate the cumulative amount for purposes of filing the first series of reports following registration of the Fund would commence with the date of the first expenditure from the Fund in connection with California elections made during the calendar year in which the Fund was so registered? 3. Might the Fund, if limited to support in connection with November elections, aggregate on a calendar-year basis? Is it permissible for the post election report following each November election to be filed with a closing date of the following December 31st if the Fund is not utilized in connection with California elections for the period between that election and the following January 1? 4. May administrative costs of the Fund be allocated if other contributions are not? Would this be permitted?
sections.conclusion: The FPPC affirms the Fund becomes a committee once it contributes $500 or more in a calendar year to California candidates. The cumulative period commences on the date of the first contribution to a California candidate during the calendar year of registration. Regarding aggregation on a calendar-year basis with December 31 closing dates, no explicit exemption is provided for corporate PACs with nationwide records maintained on a calendar basis. All contributions of $50 or more must be itemized. In-kind contributions from U.S. Steel can be calculated on a prorata basis based on the Fund's receipts contributed to California candidates, with the option to report either full or allocated disclosure, but consistency in reporting method is urged.
sections.question_synthetic: The USS Employees Good Government Fund, a corporate PAC established under federal law and primarily supporting federal candidates, seeks guidance on California Political Reform Act compliance if it begins supporting California state and local candidates, including when it becomes a committee, how to calculate cumulative reporting periods, whether it can aggregate contributions on a calendar-year basis, and whether administrative costs can be allocated separately from contributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms the Fund's committee status begins at $500 in California contributions annually and the cumulative period starts with the first California contribution in the registration year. The Commission cannot provide an exemption for corporate PACs maintaining records on a calendar basis, requires itemization of all $50+ contributions, and permits prorata allocation of in-kind administrative costs provided the method is applied consistently.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant scanning/OCR degradation throughout. The question section was reconstructed from the incoming request letter included in the document. Some specific regulatory citations are unclear due to text degradation. The FPPC response section was extracted despite formatting issues, but some precise language may be uncertain.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. When would the Fund become a "committee"? Until the Fund is utilized in connection with California elections, it cannot be said to have received any "contributions" as that term is used in Section 82103(a). A sensible approach would be to conclude that, until a total of $500 or more in a calendar year is spent from the Fund in connection with California elections, the Fund would not be considered to have received $500 or more in contributions. 2. When would the first "cumulative" period commence? The period utilized to calculate the cumulative amount for purposes of filing the first series of reports following registration of the Fund would commence with the date of the first expenditure from the Fund in connection with California elections made during the calendar year in which the Fund was so registered? 3. Might the Fund, if limited to support in connection with November elections, aggregate on a calendar-year basis? Is it permissible for the post election report following each November election to be filed with a closing date of the following December 31st if the Fund is not utilized in connection with California elections for the period between that election and the following January 1? 4. May administrative costs of the Fund be allocated if other contributions are not? Would this be permitted?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC affirms the Fund becomes a committee once it contributes $500 or more in a calendar year to California candidates. The cumulative period commences on the date of the first contribution to a California candidate during the calendar year of registration. Regarding aggregation on a calendar-year basis with December 31 closing dates, no explicit exemption is provided for corporate PACs with nationwide records maintained on a calendar basis. All contributions of $50 or more must be itemized. In-kind contributions from U.S. Steel can be calculated on a prorata basis based on the Fund's receipts contributed to California candidates, with the option to report either full or allocated disclosure, but consistency in reporting method is urged.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC response to United States Steel's August 5, 1977 inquiry regarding the applicability of California's Political Reform Act to the USS Employees Good Government Fund if it begins supporting California state and local candidates. The FPPC addresses four key questions about committee status, cumulative reporting periods, calendar-year aggregation, and allocation of administrative costs.
